From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Science and engineering award presented by the Franklin Institute

"Bower Award" redirects here. For the New Zealand award for artists, see [Olivia Spencer Bower Award](/wiki/Olivia_Spencer_Bower_Award "Olivia Spencer Bower Award").

| Franklin Institute Awards | |
| --- | --- |
| Country | United States |
| Presented by | [Franklin Institute](/wiki/Franklin_Institute "Franklin Institute") |
| First award | 1824 |
| Website | [www.fi.edu/awards](https://www.fi.edu/awards) |

The **Franklin Institute Awards** (or **Benjamin Franklin Medal**) is an American science and engineering award presented by the [Franklin Institute](/wiki/Franklin_Institute "Franklin Institute"), a science museum in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[1]](#cite_note-FranklinAwards-1) The [Franklin Institute](/wiki/Franklin_Institute "Franklin Institute") awards comprises the Benjamin Franklin Medals in seven areas of science and engineering, the Bower Awards and Prize for Achievement in Science, and the Bower Award for Business Leadership.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Since 1824, the institute has recognized "world-changing scientists, engineers, inventors, and industrialists—all of whom reflect Benjamin Franklin's spirit of curiosity, ingenuity, and innovation".[[3]](#cite_note-fi.edu-3) Some of the noted past laureates include [Nikola Tesla](/wiki/Nikola_Tesla "Nikola Tesla"), [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison"), [Marie Curie](/wiki/Marie_Curie "Marie Curie"), [Max Planck](/wiki/Max_Planck "Max Planck"), [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein"), [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking").[[3]](#cite_note-fi.edu-3) Some of the 21st century laureates of the institute awards are [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [James P. Allison](/wiki/James_P._Allison "James P. Allison"), [Indra Nooyi](/wiki/Indra_Nooyi "Indra Nooyi"), [Jane Goodall](/wiki/Jane_Goodall "Jane Goodall"), [Elizabeth Blackburn](/wiki/Elizabeth_Blackburn "Elizabeth Blackburn"), [George Church](/wiki/George_Church_(geneticist) "George Church (geneticist)"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Robert S. Langer](/wiki/Robert_S._Langer "Robert S. Langer"), and [Alex Gorsky](/wiki/Alex_Gorsky "Alex Gorsky").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Benjamin Franklin Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franklin_Institute_Awards&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Benjamin Franklin Medals")]

In 1998, the Benjamin Franklin Medals were created by reorganizing all of the endowed medals presented by The Franklin Institute at that time into a group of medals recognizing seven areas of study: Chemistry, Computer and Cognitive Science, Earth and Environmental Science, Electrical Engineering, Life Science, Mechanical Engineering, and Physics. The first Benjamin Franklin Medals were presented in 1998.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

Medalists are selected by a Committee on Science and the Arts (CS&A), composed of local academics and professionals from the Philadelphia area.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Bower Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franklin_Institute_Awards&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bower Awards")]

The Bower Award and Prize for Achievement in Science and the Bower Award for Business Leadership are the newest awards, established by a $7.5 million bequest from Henry Bower in 1988.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The annual Bower Prizes are US$250,000 each.

## Benjamin Franklin NextGen Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franklin_Institute_Awards&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Benjamin Franklin NextGen Award")]

The Benjamin Franklin NextGen Award is presented to an early-career investigator for a transformative discovery, development, innovation, or invention in science or engineering. The focus annually rotates through seven disciplines: chemistry, civil and mechanical engineering, computer and cognitive science, earth and environmental science, electrical engineering, life science, and physics.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Recipients are presented with a crystal award a cash honorarium.

## List of laureates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franklin_Institute_Awards&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: List of laureates")]

Further information: [Category:Benjamin Franklin Medal (Franklin Institute) laureates](/wiki/Category:Benjamin_Franklin_Medal_(Franklin_Institute)_laureates "Category:Benjamin Franklin Medal (Franklin Institute) laureates")

The following table lists laureates of the Benjamin Franklin Medal (including the Bower Prizewinners), from 1998 onwards.[[9]](#cite_note-Laureates_Search-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Atalla1963.png/250px-Atalla1963.png)](/wiki/File:Atalla1963.png)

[Mohamed Atalla](/wiki/Mohamed_Atalla "Mohamed Atalla"), [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") pioneer and inventor of the [MOSFET](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET") (MOS transistor), received the 1975 [Stuart Ballantine Medal](/wiki/Stuart_Ballantine_Medal "Stuart Ballantine Medal") in Physics.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Douglas_Engelbart_in_2008.jpg/250px-Douglas_Engelbart_in_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Douglas_Engelbart_in_2008.jpg)

[Douglas Engelbart](/wiki/Douglas_Engelbart "Douglas Engelbart"), inventor of the [computer mouse](/wiki/Computer_mouse "Computer mouse"), received the 1999 Benjamin Franklin Medal in Physics.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/John_McCarthy_Stanford.jpg/250px-John_McCarthy_Stanford.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_McCarthy_Stanford.jpg)

[John McCarthy](/wiki/John_McCarthy_(computer_scientist) "John McCarthy (computer scientist)"), [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") pioneer, received the 2003 Benjamin Franklin Medal in Computer Science.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Secretary_Kerry_Puts_Away_the_Benjamin_Franklin_Medal_for_Leadership_Upon_Receiving_It_in_London_%2830056450773%29.jpg/250px-Secretary_Kerry_Puts_Away_the_Benjamin_Franklin_Medal_for_Leadership_Upon_Receiving_It_in_London_%2830056450773%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Kerry_Puts_Away_the_Benjamin_Franklin_Medal_for_Leadership_Upon_Receiving_It_in_London_(30056450773).jpg)

[John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") receives the Benjamin Franklin Medal for Leadership in 2016.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Dr_Vint_Cerf_ForMemRS.jpg/250px-Dr_Vint_Cerf_ForMemRS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr_Vint_Cerf_ForMemRS.jpg)

[Vinton Cerf](/wiki/Vinton_Cerf "Vinton Cerf"), [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") [pioneer](/wiki/List_of_Internet_pioneers "List of Internet pioneers"), received the 2018 Benjamin Franklin Medal in Computer Science.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Frances_Arnold_EM1B5928_%2832361895088%29.jpg/250px-Frances_Arnold_EM1B5928_%2832361895088%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frances_Arnold_EM1B5928_(32361895088).jpg)

[Frances Arnold](/wiki/Frances_Arnold "Frances Arnold"), pioneer of [directed evolution](/wiki/Directed_evolution "Directed evolution"), received the 2019 Benjamin Franklin Medal in Chemistry.
